feed.title: Quantum Kittens
feed.subtitle: Learn quantum computing through stories about cats. A non-technical book for high school students and above.

entry.title: Title Page
entry.summary: Yaşamış ve yaşayacak, büyük küçük, tüm kediler için. Nasıl okumalı Her bölüm iki kısımdan oluşuyor: Hikaye (kısım 1) Bıyıkkent’teki kediler hakkında Yorumlama (kısım 2) hikayedeki kuantum kavramları açıklanıyor

entry.title: Chapter 1 - Story - Welcome to Whiskerton
entry.summary: Bir zamanlar, buradan çok uzakta (ama sandığınızdan daha yakın), adına Bıyıkkent denilen küçük bir yer vardı. Küçük olmasına rağmen, burası sıradanlıktan çok uzaktı. Bıyıkkent yalnızca meraklı kedilere ev sahipliği yapmakla kalmaz, kuantum fiziğinin yasalarına göre hareket ederdi. İşte bu yüzden kentin tüylü sakinleri burayı öylesine severdi. Kim, tüylerin birden belirip kaybolduğu bir yerde...

entry.title: Chapter 1 Part 2 - Commentary - Introduction to Quantum Computing

link.title: Chapter 1 Part 2 - Commentary - Introduction to Quantum Computing

entry.summary: Ours is a world governed by classical physics, in which a film is either playing or paused, a coin lands on either heads or tails, and a cat is either alive or dead. Classical physics is what we experience in our everyday lives. Based on the trajectory and velocity of a ball you’ve thrown, it’s pretty easy to predict with reasonable certainty where the ball will land–depending on your aiming sk...

entry.title: Chapter 2 - Story - Schrödinger Day
entry.summary: “Twisted yarn,” Arya cursed as she examined the Town Hall’s pantry. It was Schrödinger Day, Whiskerton’s most joyous festival, and the councilcat—with pristine ginger coat, green eyes, and a penchant for sunbathing—found herself in somewhat of a quandary. Schrödinger Day was the anniversary of Whiskerton becoming a town. The townscats always celebrated their Foundation Day with much pomp and g...

entry.title: Chapter 2 Part 2 - Commentary - Qubits, Superposition, and Measurement

link.title: Chapter 2 Part 2 - Commentary - Qubits, Superposition, and Measurement

entry.summary: You very likely know what classical computing is; it’s what most people mean when they say “computing”. This is the computing done by everyday electronic gadgets like the device you’re using to read this, and even the computing done by supercomputers tucked away in labs. Deep down at the fundamental level of a classical computer, information is carried by bits: single units of information that...
